title: Early Adieux
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Adieu to kindred hearts and home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To pleasure, joy, and mirth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A fitter foot than mine to roam
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could scarcely tread the earth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For they are now so few indeed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Not more than three in all),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who eן¿½er will think of me or heed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What fate may me befall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I through pleasureן¿½s paths have run
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My headlong goal to win,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor pleasureן¿½s snares have cared to shun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When pleasure sweetened sin.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let those who will their failings mask,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To mine I frankly own;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But for them pardon will I ask
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of none, save Heaven alone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From carping friends I turn aside;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At foes defiance frown;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet time may tame my stubborn pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And break my spirit down.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still, if to error I incline,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Truth whispers comfort strong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That never reckless act of mine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eן¿½er worked a comrade wrong.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My mother is a stately dame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who oft would chide with me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She saith my riot bringeth shame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stains my pedigree.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d reck not what my friends might know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or what the world might say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did I but think some tears would flow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I am far away.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perchance my mother will recall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My memן¿½ry with a sigh;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My gentle sisterן¿½s tears may fall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And dim her laughing eye;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps a loving thought may gleam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fringe its saddened ray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When, like a nightmareן¿½s troubled dream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, outcast, pass away.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then once again farewell to those
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoeן¿½er for me have sighed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For pleasures melt away like snows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hopes like shadows glide.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Adieu, my mother! if no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy sonן¿½s face thou mayן¿½st see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At least those many cares are oן¿½er
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So ofttimes caused by me.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My lot is fixed! The die is cast!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For me home hath no joy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, pardon then all follies past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bless your wayward boy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thou, from whom for aye to part
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grieves more than tongue can tell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ May Heaven preserve thy guileless heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweet sister, fare thee well!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou, too, whose loving-kindness makes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My resolution less,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While from the bitter past it takes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One half its bitterness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If eן¿½er you held my memן¿½ry dear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grant this request, I pray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give to that memן¿½ry one bright tear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let it pass away.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
